text:
Admission Date:  1985-12-07              Discharge Date:   1977-02-17


Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Dan
Chief Complaint:
Fall, left hip pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
ORIF left femur


History of Present Illness:
Ms. Roberts is a [**Age over 90 **]yo female with PMH significant for multiple
malignancies and HTN who presents with L femoral fracture s/p
mechanical fall. Per patient, she was in the grocery store and
was reaching for an item on the shelf when she tripped over some
boxes and fell on her L hip. No LOC at the time and she was
quickly transported to Yale-new Haven Hospital ED via EMS.
.
In the ED her initial vitals were T 97 BP 201/73 AR 104 RR 20 O2
sat 98% RA. She received nitro SL for chest pain but her blood
pressure dropped to ~66/p and she was unresponsive. She
immediately received IVFs and her BP increased appropriately.
She was transferred to the MICU for closer monitoring.
.
Patient describes 3-4 episodes of chest pain per week. She
describes the pain as an ache in her chest with no radiation but
associated with SOB. She takes a SL NTG and a russian medication
with improvement in her symptoms. She has never had a stress
test.
.


Past Medical History:
1)s/p vertebroplasty, 1974-09-20 for an L3 compression
fracture.
2)Breast cancer, status post chemotherapy.
3)Colon cancer, status post colectomy
4)Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, completed two cycles of Rituxan
treatment for her low grade lymphoma on 1995-02-28.
5)hx of gastric cancer
6)Hypertension.
7)Hypercholesterolemia.
8)s/p total left hip replacement after hip fracture.
9)Anemia.
10)Bilateral cataracts.
11)Diverticulitis.
12)Osteoporosis.
.


Social History:
Patient lives alone but has VNA services. Denies tobacco or
alcohol use. Has supportive son who lives in area.


Family History:
Non-contributory

Physical Exam:
Physical Exam:
vitals T 97 AR 62 BP 183/65 RR 21 O2 96% RA
Gen: Patient sitting up in bed, NAD
HEENT: MMM
Heart: RRR, +systolic murmur
Lungs: CTAB anteriorly
Abdomen: soft, NT/ND, +BS
Extremities: No LE edema; + swelling and tenderness from level
of L hip joint to patella.


Pertinent Results:
1985-12-07 01:10PM   PT-12.0 PTT-24.3 INR(PT)-1.0
1985-12-07 01:10PM   PLT SMR-NORMAL PLT COUNT-165
1985-12-07 01:10PM   HYPOCHROM-NORMAL ANISOCYT-NORMAL
POIKILOCY-OCCASIONAL MACROCYT-NORMAL MICROCYT-NORMAL
POLYCHROM-NORMAL BURR-OCCASIONAL
1985-12-07 01:10PM   NEUTS-66 BANDS-1 LYMPHS-28 MONOS-5 EOS-0
BASOS-0 ATYPS-0 METAS-0 MYELOS-0
1985-12-07 01:10PM   WBC-9.1# RBC-3.97* HGB-11.1* HCT-31.8*
MCV-80* MCH-27.9 MCHC-34.9 RDW-14.4
1985-12-07 01:10PM   CK-MB-NotDone cTropnT-<0.01
1985-12-07 01:10PM   CK(CPK)-70
1985-12-07 01:10PM   GLUCOSE-128* UREA N-15 CREAT-0.8 SODIUM-132*
POTASSIUM-3.3 CHLORIDE-96 TOTAL CO2-28 ANION GAP-11
1985-12-07 02:43PM   HCT-22.5*#
1985-12-07 03:33PM   HGB-9.8* calcHCT-29
1985-12-07 08:23PM   PLT COUNT-139*
1985-12-07 08:23PM   WBC-11.7* RBC-3.51* HGB-10.1* HCT-28.5*#
MCV-81* MCH-28.7 MCHC-35.4* RDW-14.3

IMAGING:
.
10-09 CXR: Cardiomegaly with left ventricular configuration.  No
acute
cardiopulmonary process.
.
10-09 Pelvis AP: Comminuted fracture of the left femoral
diaphysis distal to and not involving the left femoral
prosthesis.
.
10-09 Femur AP and Lat: There is an acute comminuted
fracture of the left femoral diaphysis distal to and not
involving the left femoral prosthesis.  Assessment of alignment
of the fragments is limited given only the AP projection.  No
other fractures are identified, although given the significant
osteopenia, assessment for subtle non-displaced fracture is
limited.  The patient is status post ORIF of bilateral proximal
femoral fractures on the right secured by dynamic compression
screw and on the left, there has been prior total hip
arthroplasty.  The left femoral component is cemented in place.
Overall the appearance of the hardware in the proximal femurs is
similar to scout image from CT 1974-09-03 and AP pelvis
1996-01-28. The sacroiliac joints and pubic symphysis are
preserved.
.
12-02 Knee X-ray:
Transverse fracture of the distal shaft of the left femur, with
posterior apex angulation.  No obvious fracture is identified
about the knee joint itself though there is associated soft
tissue swelling and probable joint effusion. No fat-fluid level
is detected (making an intra-articular fracture unlikely). There
is severe diffuse osteopenia as well as moderate degenerative
changes about the right knee.  Vascular calcification noted.
.
02-21 Femur AP and Lat (post-procedure):
A series of 18 intraoperative radiographs of the left femur
demonstrate ORIF in progress.  No immediate hardware-related
complication is seen.  The distal aspect of a left hip
prosthesis is also seen.  Displacement of the fracture fragments
is noted, although alignment of the fracture is  slightly
improved compared to the previous radiographs.  Please refer to
operative report for full details.
.
12-15 EKG: Supraventricular tachycardia which may be sinus with a
P-R interval of 0.18. There is ST-T segment depression in the
posterolateral leads. These findings are new as compared with
tracing of 1985-12-07
.
08-19 ECHO: The left atrium is normal in size. No atrial septal
defect is seen by 2D or  color Doppler. The estimated right
atrial pressure is 0-5mmHg. Left  ventricular wall thickness,
cavity size and regional/global systolic function  are normal
(LVEF >55%) Tissue Doppler imaging suggests an increased left
ventricular filling pressure (PCWP>18mmHg). There is no
ventricular septal  defect. Right ventricular chamber size and
free wall motion are normal. The  ascending aorta is mildly
dilated. The aortic valve leaflets are moderately  thickened.
There is mild aortic valve stenosis (area 1.5 cm2). Trace aortic
 regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly
thickened. There  is no mitral valve prolapse. Mild to moderate
(11-12+) mitral regurgitation is  seen. The tricuspid valve
leaflets are mildly thickened. There is mild pulmonary artery
systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion.
.
08-19: L Femur X-ray: 1.  Status post ORIF of the left distal
femur, without evidence of hardware complication.
2.  Lucency projecting over the left acetabulum likely
represents bowel gas. However, if there is concern for left
acetabular/pelvic injury, dedicated radiographs would be
recommended.


Brief Hospital Course:
A/P: Ms. Roberts is a [**Age over 90 **]yo female with PMH as listed above
presents with femur fracture s/p mechanical fall.
.
1)L femur fracture: Patient presented with left femur fracture
in the context of mechanical fall. Orthopedics service performed
ORIF L femur on 02-21 with successful result.  She was initially
started on a morphine PCA which she only used intermittently and
was switched to as needed IV morphine, with good pain control.
For DVT prophylaxis she was maintained on Lovenox.  She worked
with physical therapy soon after the surgery and was discharged
to a rehab. facility for further physical therapy.

2)Angina: Patient presented initially with symptoms consistent
with angina. She has had these symptoms for many years. Per Dr. Brenda Fernandez last note, he described her pain as a "chest pain
syndrome". Question whether her pain is cardiac in nature
although she is nitro responsive. She has not had a stress test
or prior work-up in the past.  Pt was chest pain free prior to
surgery.  On the evening following her surgery she developed a
supraventricular tachycardia and ST-segment depression with
associated chest pain. This responded quickly to po
metoprolol,she converted to normal sinus rhythm, and chest pain
resolved, however she did experience a rise in cardiac enzymes
so was started on IV heparin.  Her cardiac enzymes trended down
and she subsequently remained chest pain free.  She underwent
echocardiography subsequently, and had no wall motion
abnormalities with a preserved ejection fraction.  Her
metoprolol dose was uptitrated to 37.5mg tid, and she was
started on a full-strength aspirin. Upon discharge she was chest
pain free with no further events on telemetry.
.
3)Hypertension/Hypotension: Per OMR, patient has history of
poorly controlled hypertension and has seen Dr. Wong in the
cardiology clinic last in 1993. BP on admission elevated to
200's which came down to 180's.  Pt also with HYPOtension in the
ER after administration of nitroglycerin.  Pt has taken
nitroglycerin at home without episodes of dizziness.  However,
association of event points towards nitro as the cause of her
hypotension.  Did not receive further doses of nitroglycerin,
and blood pressure was well-controlled with increased dose of
beta-blocker.

Medications on Admission:
Atenolol 25mg PO daily
Lipitor 10mg PO daily
Ativan 1mg PO QHS
Norvasc 10mg PO daily
Zestril 20mg PO daily
Fentanyl 50 micrograms Q3 days


Discharge Medications:
1. Amlodipine 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

2. Fentanyl 50 mcg/hr Patch 72 hr Sig: One (1) Patch 72 hr
Transdermal Q72H (every 72 hours).
3. Glycerin (Adult) 3 g Suppository Sig: One (1) Suppository
Rectal PRN (as needed).
4. Atorvastatin 80 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.

5. Ciprofloxacin 250 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q12H
(every 12 hours) for 4 days.
Disp:*8 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
6. Acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO Q6H (every 6
hours) as needed for pain, temperature > 100.4.
7. Aspirin 325 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
8. Pantoprazole 40 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One
(1) Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO Q24H (every 24 hours).
9. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: 1.5 Tablets PO TID (3
times a day).
10. Enoxaparin 30 mg/0.3 mL Syringe Sig: Thirty (30) mg
Subcutaneous Q12H (every 12 hours).
11. Docusate Sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID
(2 times a day).
12. Senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO BID (2 times a day)
as needed.
13. Magnesium Hydroxide 400 mg/5 mL Suspension Sig: Thirty (30)
ML PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed.
14. Acetaminophen-Codeine 300-30 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO
Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed for pain.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Medical City Mckinney for the Aged - 329 Rodney Shores
Brucetown, WA 80120

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary:
1.) Left femur fracture
2.) Atrioventricular nodal reentrant tachycardia with associated
ischemia versus non-ST segment elevated myocardial infarction
.
Secondary:
3.) Hypertension
4.) Hypercholesterolemia
5.) Osteoporosis


Discharge Condition:
afebrile, displaying normal vital signs, tolerating po


Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital after a fall and fracture of
your left leg.  You then underwent an orthopedic surgery
involving placement of a metal device to help stabilize the
broken bone.  After the surgery you experience some chest pain
and fast heart rate which may have represented a small heart
attack.  An echocardiogram, or ultrasound of the heart after
this event showed preserved contractile function.  You were
placed on an increased dose of metoprolol, and aspirin 325 mg.
.
You will be discharged to a rehab facility in order to work on
strengthening your left leg. Upon discharge be sure to take all
new medications as instructed and continue to keep all health
care appointments.
.
If you experience severe leg pain, a fall, have chest pain or
shortness of breath, or if you have lightheadedness or
dizziness, or if your condition worsens in any way, seek
immediate medical attention.

Followup Instructions:
You have the following appointment with Dr. Wong on
06-28:
.
Provider: [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) **] Smith, MD Phone:397-133-6914
Date/Time:2008-09-11 2:00


                             Juan Edwards Eric Hanson MD 25228
